data.title: Fakeeh Care Plans Share Buyback Program
data.description:
Dr. Soliman Abdel Kader Fakeeh Hospital Co. (SASE:4017), d/b/a Fakeeh Care, plans to launch a share buyback program of up to 1,038,000 shares.

The healthcare company intends to hold the repurchased shares in the treasury, according to a Thursday filing. The repurchase requires approval from the company's extraordinary general assembly.

data.title: Tryptamine, Swinburne University Agree to Start Trial for Binge Eating Disorder Treatment Drug Candidate; Shares Rise 10%
data.description:
Tryptamine Therapeutics and Swinburne University have entered an agreement to start a clinical trial to assess the safety and efficacy of TRP-8803, a psilocin-based drug candidate for the treatment of Binge Eating Disorder (BED) along with psychotherapy, according to a Thursday filing with the Australian bourse.

The trial aims to assess the drug's safety, with secondary goals of evaluating its impact on binge-eating episodes and weight-related indicators in BED patients, with results expected by the fourth quarter of the year, the filing said.

Shares of the company rose 10% in recent Thursday trade.

data.title: China's yuan hits post financial crisis low as trade war ramps up
data.description:
Key points:
  • Yuan weakens to lowest level since 2007
  • Central bank lowers fixing for sixth session
  • Bank opting for modest, gradual yuan depreciation
  • Yuan weakness comes as Sino-US trade tension intensifies

China's yuan hit its lowest against the dollar since the global financial crisis on Thursday, with the central bank cutting guidance for the sixth successive trading session amid an intensifying Sino-U.S. trade war.

Beijing has imposed steep tariffs on U.S. imports in response to similar U.S. action. Though U.S. President Donald Trump said he would temporarily lower duties recently imposed on dozens of countries, he increased those on Chinese goods.

"The U.S. and China are currently in a powerplay game of brinkmanship," said ING global head of markets Chris Turner.

"Until a deal is announced or a big, bilateral meeting confirmed, USD/CNY will now be the focal attention of the FX market."

A weaker yuan would make Chinese exports cheaper and alleviate tariff impact on the economy. However, a sharp decline could also increase unwanted capital outflow pressure and risk financial stability, analysts and economists said.

The central bank will not allow sharp yuan declines and has instructed major state-owned lenders to reduce dollar purchases, people with knowledge of the matter told Reuters.

The onshore yuan slipped to 7.3518 a dollar in early trade, its weakest since December 26, 2007. It pared intraday losses and traded 0.02% higher at 7.3428 as of 0516 GMT, but was still down about 1.2% this month.

Its offshore counterpart was at 7.3558 at 0516 GMT, down 0.14%. It hit an all-time low of 7.4288 on Tuesday.

Prior to market open, the People's Bank of China set the midpoint - around which it allows the yuan to trade in a 2% band - at 7.2092, the weakest since September 11, 2023. That compared with the 7.3484 Reuters estimate .

The central bank has been lowering the midpoint at a measured pace, with Thursday's cut contributing to the day's decline, traders said.

The PBOC loosened its grip on the yuan this week by allowing the currency to weaken past 7.2. Still, its guidance is stronger than market projections in what traders and analysts interpreted as an attempt to keep the yuan steady.

The steadily weaker guidance dragged down its value against major trading partners. The CFETS yuan basket index (.CFSCNYI), a gauge that measures the yuan against a basket of currencies, fell to 98.18 on Thursday, the lowest since September 2024, according to Reuters calculations based on official data.

The bank is focusing on a steady yuan even as the trade war challenges the competitiveness of China's export sector, indicating that stability remains the priority.

"A modest, gradual depreciation of the yuan is still the preference," Societe Generale economists said in a client note.

China will only allow gradual depreciation as stability matters for confidence in Chinese assets, and the tariffs are "just too big to be offset by FX depreciation," they said.

Separately, China and Hong Kong shares rose on Thursday. The Hong Kong dollar hovered near a four-year high against the dollar on persistent inflows through the southbound leg of the stock trading link. It last traded at 7.7616 as of 0516 GMT.

Mainland investors purchased more than HK$35 billion ($4.51 billion) worth of Hong Kong stocks on Wednesday, the highest on record.

Marco Sun, chief financial market analyst at MUFG Bank, said a strong Hong Kong dollar was critical for the financial hub during times of heightened financial market volatility.

"And the renminbi is likely to enter a period of orderly depreciation," he said.

($1 = 7.7616 Hong Kong dollars)

data.title: U.S. Recession Looks Less Likely After Trump's Tariff PauseMarket Talk
data.description: Goldman Sachs analysts revert to their previous non-recession baseline forecast for the U.S. GDP growth, expectating it at 0.5% with a 45% probability of a recession, they say in a note. U.S. President Trump has announced a 90-day pause on certain tariffs to most countries. Before Trump's announcement, Goldman Sachs analysts had shifted to a recession baseline in response to the additional country-specific tariffs that went into effect Wednesday morning. As a result of the pause, "we are now reverting to our previous non-recession baseline forecast, which projected 2025 Q4/Q4 GDP growth of 0.5%, a peak core PCE inflation rate of 3.5%, a 45% probability of recession, and three consecutive 25bp 'insurance' cuts by the Federal Reserve in June, July, and September," the analysts say. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Cheffelo issues trading update for Q1 2025
data.description:
Cheffelo, a leading, profitable Scandinavian mealkit provider that helps people eat well without the hassle of planning and shopping, today issues a trading update for the first quarter of 2025. Full results for the period are scheduled to be announced on 7 May 2025.

Net sales for Q1 2025 amounted to MSEK 336.3 (288.7 Q1 2024), an increase of 16.5% versus last year. Adjusted for currency effects, that equals a growth of 17.6%.

The timing of Easter moving from Q1 in 2024 to Q2 in 2025 affects the comparability of Net Sales and other metrics. Excluding the affected Easter week, the growth was 14.0% adjusted for currency effects.

Active customers for the first quarter were 86.8 thousand (81.5 Q1 2024), an increase of 6.5% versus last year.

Cheffelo will invite investors, analysts and the media to a conference call and webcast with Q&A, which will take place on the day the Q1 results are published.

data.title: Japan to Keep Pushing for Tariff Relief After US Concessions
data.description:
Japan will continue pressing the US to review its tariff measures, hoping for relief after President Trump paused some reciprocal tariffs, said Economic Revitalisation Minister Ryosei Akazawa.

Tokyo, still facing tariffs on cars, auto parts, steel, and aluminum, sees the pause as a positive step and expects to be prioritized in upcoming trade talks.

US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent plans to meet officials from Japan, Vietnam, India, and South Korea soon.

Akazawa, recently appointed to lead the negotiations, emphasized Japan's intent to seek further tariff reductions.

data.title: European Markets Head Higher on US Tariff Relief
data.description:
European equity markets were poised for a strong open on Thursday, buoyed by news that U.S. President Donald Trump will reduce new tariff rates to 10% for most U.S. trade partners over a 90-day period to allow for negotiations.

The move marks a significant shift from the previously imposed 20% tariff on European Union imports.

However, uncertainty remains over whether the EU will be included in the temporary tariff reprieve, as the bloc recently announced its first round of countermeasures in response to U.S. trade actions.

In premarket trading, futures for the Euro Stoxx 50 and Stoxx 600 surged approximately 8% and 7%, respectively, signaling a strong rebound in investor sentiment across the region.

data.title: China Sanjiang Fine Chemicals' Management Contract Falls Through
data.description:
China Sanjiang Fine Chemicals has terminated an agreement to procure management services from Grand Novel Developments, a Wednesday filing with the Hong Kong bourse said.

The move came about as there is uncertainty regarding the logistic arrangement in the procurement of ethane, propane, butane, methanol, and silver among others amid tariffs imposed by the US.

The parties had entered into the management services contract in January, according to the chemicals company.

data.title: U.S. Treasury Yields Drop, Volatility PersistsMarket Talk
data.description:
0535 GMT - U.S. Treasury yields drop in the early European trading hours, reversing from Wednesday's double-digit increases. The rise in yields was prompted by news that U.S. President Trump announced a 90-day pause on certain tariffs for most countries, even as he raised tariffs on China to 125%. "Despite the calming winds, implied volatility in fixed income markets will likely remain elevated as the administration's next move remains very unpredictable," Danske Bank Research's Frederik Romedahl says in a note. The two-year Treasury yield is 5.5 basis points lower at 3.893%; the 10-year Treasury yield is down 11 basis points at 4.287% and the 30-year Treasury yield is also 10 basis points lower at 4.693%, according to Tradeweb data. Trading volumes in these hours are likely thinner. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0528 GMT - Higher U.S. tariffs on China will likely weigh significantly on the Chinese economy and labor market, Goldman Sachs economists say. "We are revising our real GDP growth forecasts for 2025 and 2026 downward to 4.0% and 3.5%, respectively, from our previous projections of 4.5% and 4.0%," they say. President Trump's additional tariff hikes are likely to have a diminishing marginal impact, but the steep increase is still set to weigh on the economy. GS expects Beijing to intensify policy easing, and now sees 60 bps of policy rate cuts versus 40 bps previously. It also anticipates a 4.1 ppt expansion in its estimate of the "augmented fiscal deficit" this year to 14.5% of its GDP. "However, even these significant easing measures are unlikely to fully offset the negative effects of the tariffs," they say. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0516 GMT - With a high degree of uncertainty surrounding U.S. trade policy likely to persist through early July, the Bank of Japan is expected to hold interest rates steady at its next meeting on April 30 and May 1, Morgan Stanley MUFG Securities economists say. "The BOJ will need to assess the negative impact of the 25% autos tariff on SME [small and medium enterprise} wage negotiations taking place over April-June," they say in a research note. The economists see the next rate increase as most likely in September, with some probability of a rate rise in July, depending on forex moves and the upper house election results. They expect the BOJ to pause monetary tightening after raising the policy rate to 0.75% from the current 0.5%. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

0503 GMT - Bear flattening pressure persists in U.S. Treasurys, as markets trim Fed rate-cut expectations, analysts say. The latest surge in the two-year yield comes amid paring of Fed rate-cut bets, DBS Group Research's Eugene Leow says in commentary. "Meanwhile, the rapid selloff in long end U.S. (Treasurys) also cooled as market dysfunction eased somewhat," the senior rates strategist says. A bear flattening typically refers to short-term yields rising faster than long-dated yields on expectations of tighter monetary policy. Two-year Treasury yield is up 3bps at 3.8932%, while 10-year yield is down 1bp at 4.2855%. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0458 GMT - As the Japanese government likely has a strong incentive to reach a deal with President Trump before the Upper House election due by late July, the negotiations between the two countries are expected to be settled within the 90-day tariff pause, Morgan Stanley MUFG Securities economists say in a research note. However, there remain downside risks for the Japanese economy as the unpredictability of U.S. tariff policy could weigh on capital spending globally, including that by Japanese companies, they say.(megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

0448 GMT - China's exports are set to face strong headwinds, even as the domestic economy may show signs of recovery prior to implementation of major stimulus, says Wenli Zheng, portfolio manager of China evolution equity strategy at T. Rowe Price. Exports constitute 18% of China's GDP, with direct exports to the U.S. accounting for 15% of overall shipments, which could translate to around 2.7% of China's GDP, or 3%-3.5% if including trade diversions and re-exports of intermediate goods, Zheng notes. China has greater policy flexibility than before as it has been de-leveraging from the property sector, which would boost consumption and investment-related sectors, he says. The shift to supporting the domestic economy isn't solely because of tariffs, but reflects a trend that is expected to continue over the next year or two, he adds. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0445 GMT - Beijing will likely prioritize stabilizing the domestic financial markets amid the intensifying trade war with the U.S., Nomura analysts write in a note. Premier Li Qiang said at a symposium of economic experts and entrepreneurs that it is necessary to implement more proactive macroeconomic policies to address the external shocks, they note. Beijing imposing tariffs on the U.S. has shown that China will retaliate commensurately, Nomura says. China's stabilization funds, known as the national teams, have intervened substantially in the domestic stock market over the past two days, the analysts say. They expect China to ensure stabilization of its financial markets during the trade war. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0405 GMT - The escalating U.S.-China tariff conflict isn't likely to drive further damage to trade, given that current levies are already prohibitive, but risks of a broader spillover persist without signs of de-escalation, Citi analysts say in a research note. "In the current situation, we share the concern that meaningful negotiation may not resume before serious damage is done, "the analysts say. Ongoing efforts by Beijing to stabilize equities and forex markets should cap investor worries, they add. Citi also thinks there is a higher chance of domestic stimulus being brought forward, and expects China's fiscal policy to drive expansion in domestic demand given external shocks. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0336 GMT - The exclusion of China from the U.S.'s 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs suggests a breakdown in negotiations between the two countries, DBS Group Research's Chang Wei Liang says in a commentary. China retaliated to further U.S. levies by raising its tariff on U.S. goods to 84% on Wednesday, prompting President Trump to increase duties on Chinese imports to 125%, the foreign-exchange and credit strategist notes. "Markets are closely watching whether China would allow a softening in the RMB to cushion tariff shocks," he says. USD/CNH is 0.1% higher at 7.3557, while USD/CNY is 0.1% lower at 7.3443, according to FactSet. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0333 GMT - China's state-run newspaper Securities Daily says now is an appropriate time to cut interest rates and banks' reserve requirement ratio, suggesting authorities may hand out more monetary easing in the face of Trump's tariff hikes. In a front-page article Thursday, the newspaper says there is still room to lower the RRR, which currently stands at 6.6% on average, and borrowing costs of funds provided by the central bank via its structural monetary policy tools. Such easing could alleviate sudden external shocks, send out strong policy signals, help boost confidence and stabilize market expectations, it says. (singaporeeditors@dowjones.com)

0245 GMT - The Singapore dollar weakens against its U.S. counterpart in the Asian session, weighed by abating prospects for Fed rate cuts. The FOMC meeting's minutes released overnight show the bulk of policymakers see inflation could be more persistent, with risks tilted to the upside, MUFG Bank's Lloyd Chan says in a research report. "This, along with the tariff relief, has led to markets paring back some of their Fed rate cut expectation," the senior currency analyst says. Diminishing expectations for Fed rate cuts typically bolster the appeal of U.S. fixed-income assets and demand for the greenback. USD/SGD is 0.2% higher at 1.3426. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0242 GMT - The latest selling of yen, triggered by President Trump's pause on so-called reciprocal tariffs for some countries, is unlikely to continue, SMBC's Hirofumi Suzuki says. Continuing tariffs on China and 10% levies for others "are expected to have a visible impact on economic fundamentals in the near future," the chief forex strategist says. "The uncertainty that once intensified in the financial markets is unlikely to dissipate easily," he says, adding that wide price swings are likely to continue for some time. USD/JPY is 0.6% lower at 146.83. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.description:

data.title: Givaudan Sales Grow; Warns of Rising Prices
data.description:
By Adam Whittaker

Givaudan said that first-quarter sales grew but warned of higher prices caused by growing costs and tariffs which will be passed on to customers.

The Swiss flavor-and-fragrance company said Thursday that first-quarter sales rose 8.5% to 1.98 billion Swiss francs ($2.31 billion) compared with the same period a year prior.

Givaudan said it is raising prices due to higher costs and tariffs, and intends for the price increases to fully compensate for the rise in input costs.

Givaudan remains focused on delivering its strategy despite uncertainty caused by global trade tariffs, the company's Chief Executive Gilles Andrier said.

The company said it has started the year with strong momentum, delivering growth across markets and segments. Sales in high-growth markets continued to grow but at a slower pace, increasing 12.8% on a like-for-like basis compared with 21.8% a year earlier.

Givaudan backed its sales targets and said it expects to exceed the upper end of its average five-year sales growth target of 4%-5% on a like-for-like basis for the period 2021-25. It had averaged like-for-like sales growth of 7.2% between 2021 and 2024, it said.

Sales within its fragrance-and-beauty division increased 12.2% to 1.01 billion Swiss francs, and rose 5% to 968 million Swiss francs in its taste-and-wellbeing division.

Write to Adam Whittaker at adam.whittaker@wsj.com

data.title: Alisa Bank, Fennoa Launch Business Banking Services
data.description:
Alisa Bank and financial management software company Fennoa partnered to offer business banking services.

The Finnish digital lender will offer the Fennoa Payment Package to Fennoa's clients, which are primarily accounting companies, according to a Thursday release. The fixed-price, add-on service connects Alisa Bank with Fennoa's financial management software and is slated to launch by the end of 2025.

The service authorizes Fennoa to deliver payment materials, receive payment feedback, and retrieve account statements and real-time balances for its business clients without additional fees.

data.title: Skanska To Replace Clifton Bridge In North England, UK
data.description:
Skanska AB :

  • REPLACES CLIFTON BRIDGE IN NORTH ENGLAND, UK, FOR GBP 43M, ABOUT SEK 580M

data.title: Berner Kantonalbank Creates New Market Services, Innovation/Digitalization Departments
data.description:
Berner Kantonalbank said Thursday it set up Market Services and Innovation/Digitalization departments to strengthen customer-facing initiatives and improve efficiency.

The Swiss cantonal bank said the Market Services department will merge its customer center and sales and infrastructure services to support its sales.

Meanwhile, the Innovation/Digitalization unit will consolidate all IT-related areas to drive innovation and digitalization programs and secure its digital infrastructure.

data.title: Salik Resumes Trading on Dubai Bourse
data.description:
Salik (DFM:SALIK) recommenced trading on the Dubai Financial Market on Thursday, according to a same-day notice.

The trading suspension started Wednesday and was lifted after the tollgate operator published its general assembly resolutions.

data.title: REG-Transaction in Own Shares
data.description:
ENDEAVOUR ANNOUNCES TRANSACTION IN OWN SHARES

London, 10 April 2025 – Endeavour Mining plc (LSE:EDV, TSX:EDV) (“the Company”) announces it has purchased the following number of its ordinary shares of USD 0.01 each from Stifel Nicolaus Europe Limited.

Aggregated information

Dates of purchase:09 April 2025
Aggregate number of ordinary shares of USD 0.01 each purchased:30,000
Lowest price paid per share (GBp):                1,759.01
Highest price paid per share (GBp):        1,780.00
Volume weighted average price paid per share (GBp):        1,767.80

Following the cancellation of the repurchased shares, the Company will have no ordinary shares in treasury and 241,872,483 ordinary shares in issue. Therefore the total voting rights in the Company will be 241,872,483. This figure for the total number of voting rights may be used by shareholders as the denominator for the calculations by which they will determine if they are required to notify their interest in, or a change to their interest in, the Company under the FCA's Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules.

These share purchases form part of the Company's buy-back programme announced on 20 March 2024.

ABOUT ENDEAVOUR MINING PLC

Endeavour Mining is one of the world’s senior gold producers and the largest in West Africa, with operating assets across Senegal, Cote d’Ivoire and Burkina Faso and a strong portfolio of advanced development projects and exploration assets in the highly prospective Birimian Greenstone Belt across West Africa.

A member of the World Gold Council, Endeavour is committed to the principles of responsible mining and delivering sustainable value to its employees, stakeholders and the communities where it operates. Endeavour is admitted to listing and to trading on the London Stock Exchange and the Toronto Stock Exchange, under the symbol EDV.

data.title: He's One of Wall Street's Biggest Bears. Where He's Investing Now.
data.description:
By Paul R. La Monica

David Rosenberg has long been one of the more bearish strategists on Wall Street. At Merrill Lynch in the 2000s, he was one of the few to predict the global financial crisis of 2008-09. Since then, he has largely maintained a negative outlook, intermingled with bullish calls on various assets and markets.

With his reputation as a "permabear," one might think Rosenberg would be taking a victory lap. Financial markets are in turmoil and, despite a surge on Wednesday, the S&P 500 is still down since President Donald Trump announced sweeping additional tariffs on April 3. Volatility has surged and Treasury yields have been spiking as investors worry about a mix of higher inflation and slowing growth — the dreaded specter of "stagflation."

Yet Rosenberg — who runs his own investment firm, Rosenberg Research & Associates, in his hometown of Toronto — does see pockets of strength. He shifted his firm's outlook recently from Bearish to Neutral, citing depressed sentiment and oversold technicals. He isn't upbeat on the S&P 500 seeing the index in a range of 4,600-5,300 over the next 12 months. More attractive, he says, are European defense stocks, Asian equities, and gold.

As for Trump's whipsawing policies, Rosenberg isn't convinced the president's tariff pause will be a game-changer. "Let's not forget that the trade war with China is moving into high gear," he said.

"Households and corporations crave policy stability and aren't receiving it," he added. "If anything, all the president has done is make the overall economic backdrop that much more confusing."

Rosenberg, 64, spoke this week with Barron's about his views on tariffs, the financial markets, and the economy. An edited version of the conversation follows.

Barron's: Your latest stock market call is more optimistic than usual. Why the change?

David Rosenberg: A lot of sentiment metrics in this correction phase have gotten completely washed out. That paves the way for a near-term tradable rally. Whenever the VIX pops above 40 [the volatility measure topped 50 earlier this week], 96% of the time the S&P 500 is up in the ensuing 12 months. If you have that degree of staying power, this [could be] a good buying opportunity.

I'm not convinced that the market lows are in. You may have to hold your nose and withstand a move to a cycle low, which is probably two months away. It depends on your tolerance for short-term pain and how much resolve you have.

Investors don't like Trump's tariffs. Is the White House making a big mistake?

Trump has been opposed to bilateral trade surpluses with the U.S. for almost 40 years. His view on trade, in general, is that other countries' surpluses are an attempt to rip off Americans. For Trump, this is like religion.

I don't view trade surpluses or deficits as good or bad. They're just what they are. I roll my eyes and shake my head when hearing about the perils of running trade deficits. They show the relative strength of the U.S. economy. You should wear that as a badge of courage.

This is a massive assault on a beast otherwise known as global trade. There are going to be repercussions. There are only losers in a trade war, and we have only winners when we have global trade flow. But globalism has become a repulsive term. We're now on the precipice of a global trade war, and that would be catastrophic.

You've written that the market can move to excesses in both directions and make faulty assumptions, but "the market is not stupid." What faulty assumptions are investors making now?

At the time I wrote that [in December 2024], I said I underestimated the power of the artificial-intelligence trade. But the market can go from a mania into a bubble. Stocks, at their peak, were embedding a five-year profile of 20% earnings growth, almost triple the annual average rate of 7%. There is historically a one-in-20 chance of that occurring.

But it wasn't about earnings; it was about the price/earnings multiple. That is the pulse of animal spirits and investor confidence. Did I think we'd get to 23 or 24 times earnings for the market? No.

Heading into the peak, I was wrong in not sharing the market's assumptions. But that was then, and this is now.

What would it take for you to go from being neutral on equities to bullish? When was the last time you were optimistic on the U.S. stock market?

People don't seem to remember that I was very bullish on many segments of the market coming out of the March 2020 lows. I said then that I liked anything remotely connected to tech, such as working from home and deliveries. I also turned bullish from about 2012 to 2017. So, I was in about 60% of that bull market after the global financial crisis.

I may be typically late to a bull market, but I never overstay my welcome. I'm deliberately slow to get in and early to get out. But as a result, in my 40-year career, I've never lost money over a 12-month span.

Were your clients annoyed with you for missing out on some gains?

I've had clients yelling at me that I wasn't being truthful when I liked stocks. They thought it was like taking John Wayne out of a Western and putting him in a romantic comedy. But I was bullish through most of the 1980s and 1990s. By the late '90s, I saw the tech bubble for what it was and became more bearish.

But I am deliberately conservative. I can't stand losing money. Capital preservation is the First Commandment. I was brought up with Depression-era parents who taught me about frugality and savings.

I'll bunt for singles and be happy with the odd double. But I'll never swing for the fences. I don't hit home runs, but I also don't strike out. With today's get-rich-quick philosophy promulgated in so many circles, minimizing drawdowns somehow has labeled me a permabear. That's more a reflection of society than what it says about me.

How much longer do you expect the Federal Reserve to remain on the sidelines before cutting interest rates again?

The Fed is caught in a box between the prospect of much slower growth and a near-term inflation shock. But I've got news for you: If there is no inflation in the jobs market, there is no lasting inflation. The difference between this inflation shock and 2021-22 is that this time we will hit a wall in the labor market.

The Fed is suffering from a case of once burned, twice shy. It will be slow to cut rates again. If not for getting things wrong about inflation being transitory, it would probably be cutting now. But the Fed is consumed with fears about its credibility. [Fed Chair Jerome] Powell's term ends in a year, and given a choice, he'd rather go down presiding over a recession than inflation. Paul Volcker was revered for finally breaking the back of inflation in the 1980s. So, the Fed will be deliberately slow.

When the time comes to cut again, the Fed will be aggressive in the second half of the year. It will be late to the game. That won't be the first time. It will be scrambling to cut rates as odds of a recession have risen materially. Recession risks are high and rising. I know that sounds like 'The Boy Who Cried Wolf." But remember that at the end of that story, the wolf shows up.

Why are recession risks high? The labor market still looks healthy.

Excess savings from Covid are all gone. Besides that, the unprecedented period of fiscal stimulus we had is over, and fiscal policy isn't stimulative due to Department of Government Efficiency, or DOGE, cuts and Elon Musk. There is no safety valve or powerful offsets to what is happening now.

Trade policy is being conducted haphazardly. On the one side, some are saying tariffs are permanent; others are saying we'll negotiate. There is this bog of policy uncertainty that is causing us to become more risk-averse. This bog, if it persists, will shave percentage points off gross domestic product for the coming year.

What does all of this mean for other assets, such as international stocks and currencies, bonds, oil, and gold?

Europe and Asia still look superior. European defense stocks are a good place to be. And even though I don't make currency calls too often, we went long the Japanese yen about a month ago. If there is a country in the world that has inflation, it's Japan. Their central bank will be raising rates. You could have equity-like returns being long the yen. That is our highest-conviction call. Asia in general looks attractively priced. It has the valuation support that the U.S. is still lacking.

For bonds, if I am right that the Fed will cut again in the second half of the year, I like the Treasury market, especially longer duration like the 20-year. Oil has gone down due to OPEC+ production and recession concerns. But it is coming closer to an attractive level.

On gold, I've been bullish for years — long before it became front-page news. Our models point to gold being a range-bound trade in 2025, but in the longer term we're still very bullish. Gold could double over the next five years. It is always a ballast in a portfolio, and it is inversely correlated to other assets, so it is an effective hedge.

Also, if you reset global trade and want to talk about the laws of unintended consequences, for the first time in my life, the U.S. could relinquish its reserve currency status. That is on the table.

What would replace the dollar is an open question, but this is the road we are being led down. And if we start to have this reset of global trade, a $6,000 view on gold within the next five years could wind up being a conservative estimate. So, I still like gold. I don't love it, but I probably will end up loving it again.

Thank you, David.

Write to Paul R. La Monica at paul.lamonica@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Skanska replaces Clifton bridge in north England, UK, for GBP 43M, about SEK 580M
data.description:
Skanska

Skanska has signed a contract with Network Rail for its replacement Clifton bridge project in north England, UK. The contract is worth GBP 43M, about SEK 580M, which will be included in the order bookings for Europe for the second quarter 2025.

Skanska will replace the Clifton rail bridge that spans the M6 motorway near Penrith, UK. The existing bridge will be demolished and replaced with a new 130 meter single span steel structure. The new bridge will be built alongside the existing structure, before being installed over a 10-day period early January 2026. Existing bridge support structures will be re-used, reducing programme timescales, cost and resultant carbon emissions.

Construction works have started with the project scheduled for completion in March 2026.

For further information please contact:

David Tuddenham, Senior Communications Business Partner, Skanska UK, tel +44 7880 406018

Andreas Joons, Press Officer, Skanska Group, tel +46 (0)10 449 04 94

Direct line for media, tel +46 (0)10 448 88 99

This and previous releases can also be found at www.skanska.com.

Skanska Group uses its knowledge and foresight to shape the way people live, work, and connect. We are one of the world's largest construction and project development companies, with 2024 revenue totaling SEK 177 billion. We operate across select markets in the Nordics, Europe and USA. Together with our customers and the collective expertise of our approximately 26,300 teammates, we create innovative and sustainable solutions that support healthy living beyond our lifetime.
